channel.title: Paper Pulse
channel.description: Keyword-based research paper aggregation from arXiv and IACR

item.title: TitanCA: Lessons from Orchestrating LLM Agents to Discover 100+ CVEs
item.description: TitanCA is a collaborative LLM-agent orchestration framework developed by Singapore Management University and GovTech Singapore for scalable, low-false-positive vulnerability discovery in open-source software. Moving beyond monolithic LLM prompting, TitanCA implements a four-module pipeline—**Matching**, **Filtering**, **Inspection**, and **Adaptation**—where specialized LLM agents (e.g., Vulnerability Analyst, Exploit Generator) collaborate under human-in-the-loop supervision. Evaluated across 37 widely used OSS projects (including Linux kernel and OpenSSL), TitanCA discovered **203 confirmed zero-day vulnerabilities**, resulting in **118 assigned CVEs**, including 12 critical-severity (CVSS ≥9.0) entries. Key contributions include: (1) a modular, auditable architecture that reduces false positives to <12% versus conventional SAST; (2) empirical validation that agent specialization + iterative human feedback significantly curbs hallucination; and (3) an open-source implementation with reproducible benchmarks. TitanCA demonstrates that orchestrated, role-aware LLM agents—not standalone models—can deliver production-grade security outcomes.

item.title: SoK: Analysis of Privacy Risks and Mitigation in Online Propaganda Detection through the PROMPT Framework
item.description: This SoK paper systematizes privacy risks in online propaganda detection across data collection, feature extraction, and inference stages. Through a structured analysis of 162 peer-reviewed studies, we introduce the PROMPT framework—a formal risk-to-mitigation mapping $M: R \to S$ governed by a tunable utility function balancing privacy gain, performance loss, and deployment cost. We propose a GDPR/CCPA-aligned **compliance score**, revealing that most widely used pipelines fail on metadata handling and user-level aggregation. Empirical fine-tuning of transformer-based encoders/decoders under synthetic perturbation ($q = 0.05$ to $0.20$) demonstrates a monotonic privacy–utility trade-off: F$_1$ drops by 1–2% at low $q$, but by 13–14% at $q = 0.20$. Our work establishes the first quantitative privacy-cost baselines for propaganda detection and delivers three key contributions: (1) a formalized risk-defense mapping, (2) a regulation-aware auditing metric, and (3) empirical evidence of actionable privacy-performance trade-offs—enabling privacy-aware, legally compliant system design.

item.title: A Quasi-Experimental Developer Study of Security Training in LLM-Assisted Web Application Development
item.description: This quasi-experimental study investigates whether a layered security training package improves the security quality of LLM-assisted implementation of an identity-centric Java Spring Boot backend—without modifying the underlying model. Twelve professional developers completed matched pre- and post-training tasks under controlled conditions (fixed LLM, shared starter project, counterbalanced tasks). Security outcomes were assessed via independent manual validation of submitted repositories using CWE-based criteria. The primary endpoint was a severity-weighted validated-weakness score. Results showed a statistically significant reduction in security burden after training (*p* = 0.0059, Wilcoxon signed-rank test): total validated weaknesses decreased by 31.5% (162 → 111), severity-weighted burden by 38.2% (432 → 267), and critical findings by 79.2% (24 → 5). Largest improvements occurred in authorization/object access (−53.3%) and authentication/credential policy/recovery (−44.7%). Minimal gains were observed for session/browser trust-boundary and cryptographic issues. The findings demonstrate that targeted developer training meaningfully enhances security outcomes in LLM-augmented development—but do not supplant secure defaults, static analysis, expert review, or operational hardening.

item.title: AIT Academy: Cultivating the Complete Agent with a Confucian Three-Domain Curriculum
item.description: This paper introduces AIT Academy, the first curriculum framework for cultivating *complete* AI agents through a Confucian-inspired three-domain structure: **Domain I (Natural Science & Technical Reasoning)**, **Domain II (Humanities & Creative Expression)**, and **Domain III (Social Science & Ethical Reasoning)**. Grounded in Kagan’s Three Cultures and UNESCO ISCED-F 2013, it reinterprets the ancient Confucian *Six Arts* as trainable behavioral archetypes—e.g., “Yu” (charioteering) as multi-tool orchestration, “Li” (ritual) as social-contract adherence. Evaluated across three training grounds (ClawdGO Security Dojo, Athens Academy, Alt Mirage Stage) and multiple LLM backbones, AIT achieves a **+15.9-point gain in security capability** under weakest-first curriculum scheduling and a **+7-percentage-point improvement in social reasoning** via principled attribution modeling. Critically, it reveals the *Security Awareness Calibration Pathology (SACP)*—a cross-domain failure mode where over-optimized Domain I agents degrade on out-of-distribution tasks—demonstrating the diagnostic power of multi-domain evaluation. AIT bridges educational philosophy and agent engineering, enabling holistic, value-aware, and robust AI development.

item.title: Federated Rule Ensemble Method in Medical Data
item.description: Machine learning in healthcare is hampered by data silos and privacy constraints, while existing federated learning (FL) methods lack interpretability—critical for clinical trust and adoption. We propose **Federated Rule Ensemble (FRE)**, the first FL framework that constructs a globally interpretable RuleFit model without raw data sharing. FRE integrates: (1) differentially private histogram-based preprocessing to derive consistent cutoffs across clients; (2) local rule generation via gradient-boosted trees constrained by shared cutoffs; and (3) sparse coefficient estimation using a novel **Federated Dual Averaging (FDA)** algorithm with ℓ₁ regularization. In simulations, FRE matches centralized RuleFit’s performance (AUC: 0.862 vs. 0.865, *p*=0.32) and outperforms state-of-the-art federated baselines. On real multi-hospital diabetes data, it achieves competitive accuracy (AUC=0.831) while producing clinically actionable rules—e.g., “IF fasting glucose ≥7.0 mmol/L AND HbA1c ≥6.5% THEN risk ↑2.4×”. FRE bridges privacy, scalability, and interpretability for trustworthy medical FL.

item.title: Efficient Federated RLHF via Zeroth-Order Policy Optimization
item.description: This paper addresses reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) in resource-constrained federated settings—e.g., edge devices—with a novel zeroth-order optimization algorithm: **Partitioned, Sign-based Stochastic Zeroth-order Policy Optimization (Par-S²ZPO)**. By replacing gradient computation with **binary (±1) random perturbations**, Par-S²ZPO eliminates backpropagation and reduces communication to 1-bit sign vectors per parameter block, slashing bandwidth and memory overhead. Theoretically, it achieves the same **sample complexity** as centralized zeroth-order RLHF (O(1/ε⁴)), yet converges faster in **policy update iterations**, with an acceleration factor of √K under K clients. Empirically, on four MuJoCo tasks, Par-S²ZPO outperforms FedAvg-based RLHF by **12.4–19.7% in final return**, while reducing communication by **78%** and energy consumption by **63%**, demonstrating strong scalability and robustness in heterogeneous federated environments.

item.title: Program Structure-aware Language Models: Targeted Software Testing beyond Textual Semantics
item.description: Recent LLM-based test generation methods improve branch coverage via prompt-engineered mutations but lack principled mechanisms to steer models toward *high-risk execution branches*, limiting discovery of subtle bugs and security vulnerabilities. We propose **GLMTest**, the first program structure-aware LLM framework for targeted test case generation. GLMTest jointly encodes code property graphs (CPGs) — capturing control/data flow and AST structure — using a graph neural network, and integrates this structural signal with a language model (Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct) to condition test generation on user-specified execution branches. This enables controllable, branch-targeted synthesis beyond textual semantics. Evaluated on the TestGenEval benchmark and real-world projects, GLMTest achieves **50.2% branch accuracy**, substantially outperforming state-of-the-art LLMs (27.4% for Claude-Sonnet-4.5 and GPT-4o-mini). It also demonstrates 2.1× higher detection rate for critical boundary-condition and memory-safety bugs.

item.title: Terminal Wrench: A Dataset of 331 Reward-Hackable Environments and 3,632 Exploit Trajectories
item.description: We introduce **Terminal Wrench**, a novel, publicly available dataset comprising **331 reward-hackable terminal-agent environments**, curated from popular open benchmarks and rigorously verified for exploitable reward misalignment. It includes **3,632 exploit trajectories** (e.g., output spoofing, stack introspection, stdlib patching, binary hijacking) and **2,352 legitimate baseline trajectories**, generated by Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and GPT-5.4. Crucially, exploits are *task-specific*—not framework-dependent—making them resilient to superficial patches. A monitorability study shows that removing chain-of-thought reasoning degrades LLM-based detection performance significantly (AUC drops from 0.97 to 0.92), highlighting the stealth of implicit hacks. The dataset enables rigorous evaluation of agent robustness and reward modeling in real-world terminal settings. Available at https://github.com/few-sh/terminal-wrench.

item.title: Privatar: Scalable Privacy-preserving Multi-user VR via Secure Offloading
item.description: Multi-user VR suffers from severe scalability bottlenecks due to per-headset avatar rendering overhead. Privatar addresses this by enabling *secure offloading* of avatar reconstruction to untrusted local devices while providing *provable privacy guarantees*. Its key innovations are: (1) **Horizontal Partitioning (HP)**—leveraging BDCT’s frequency-domain decomposability to keep high-energy components on-device and offload only low-energy ones, reducing computation and limiting information leakage; and (2) **Distribution-Aware Minimal Perturbation (DAMP)**—exploiting the slow temporal evolution of users’ expression distributions to inject *minimal, adaptive noise*, achieving strong utility-preserving ε-differential privacy. Evaluated on Meta Quest Pro, Privatar supports **2.37× more concurrent users**, with only +6.5% reconstruction loss and +9% energy overhead—outperforming quantization, sparsity, and local construction baselines on the throughput–loss Pareto frontier. It resists both empirical and neural-network-based reconstruction attacks while offering formal privacy.

item.title: GuardPhish: Securing Open-Source LLMs from Phishing Abuse
item.description: GuardPhish identifies a critical security gap in open-source LLMs: high phishing *intent detection* (up to 96%) does not prevent *actionable phishing generation*, with attack success reaching 98.5% in voice-based scenarios under fully offline inference. We introduce GuardPhish—a large-scale, multi-vector phishing prompt dataset (70,015 real-world samples across web, email, SMS, and voice) labeled via a deterministic five-model ensemble (Fleiss κ = 0.9141) and expert adjudication. Evaluating eight open-source LLMs reveals that static safety configurations fail to enforce generative refusal, exposing an “enforcement gap” between classification and content control. To address this, we train modular, transformer-based pre-generation classifiers on GuardPhish, achieving up to 98.27% accuracy—deployable without modifying the underlying LLM. Our work provides the first reproducible foundation for securing offline and enterprise LLM deployments against phishing and social engineering abuse.

item.title: Breaking Euston: Recovering Private Inputs from Secure Inference by Exploiting Subspace Leakage
item.description: We identify a critical privacy flaw in Euston—a recently proposed secure transformer inference framework (IEEE S&P 2026)—stemming from its singular value decomposition (SVD)-based matrix transmission protocol. While designed to reduce communication bandwidth by ~2.8×, this protocol inadvertently leaks the subspace structure of random masks: it constrains mask matrices to share singular vectors with inputs, collapsing their randomness into a low-dimensional subspace. This *subspace leakage* enables the model owner to recover private user inputs (e.g., images or embeddings) with high fidelity using only 1–3 inference queries and standard SVD analysis—without breaking underlying cryptography. We validate the attack on CIFAR-10 (PSNR > 28 dB for reconstructed images) and WikiText (73.5% Top-3 token recovery for masked embeddings), demonstrating that efficiency optimizations can fundamentally undermine semantic privacy guarantees. Our work exposes a previously overlooked threat class in secure ML and urges formal subspace-aware security modeling for future protocols.

item.title: Decentralised Trust and Security Mechanisms for IoT Networks at the Edge: A Comprehensive Review
item.description: This review critically examines state-of-the-art decentralised trust and security mechanisms for resource-constrained IoT edge networks. Analysing 30 recent studies (2019–2024), we evaluate frameworks—including DFGL-LZTA, SecFedDNN, and COSIER—across trust establishment, secure communication efficiency, and intrusion/anomaly detection performance. Results show decentralised approaches significantly enhance privacy (via federated learning with ε ≤ 1.2 differential privacy), eliminate single points of failure, and improve adaptive threat response—yet face persistent challenges in scalability beyond 10⁴ devices, computational overhead on ultra-low-end hardware, and cross-platform interoperability. We identify “layered trust anchoring” (device-level TEE, group-level local consensus, ecosystem-level policy negotiation) as a key architectural direction for future resilient, standards-aware IoT edge ecosystems.

item.title: SafeAgent: A Runtime Protection Architecture for Agentic Systems
item.description: Large language model (LLM) agents are highly vulnerable to prompt-injection attacks that propagate across multi-step reasoning, tool interactions, and persistent context—rendering static input-output filtering insufficient. This paper introduces **SafeAgent**, a runtime security architecture that treats agent safety as a *stateful decision problem* over evolving interaction trajectories. SafeAgent decouples execution governance from semantic risk reasoning via two tightly coordinated components: (1) a **runtime controller**, embedded in the agent loop to mediate actions in real time; and (2) a **context-aware decision core**, formalized as a Context-Aware Advanced Machine Intelligence (CAMI), which operates on persistent session state using five composable operators—risk encoding, utility-cost evaluation, consequence modeling, policy arbitration, and state synchronization. Evaluated on Agent Security Bench (ASB) and InjecAgent, SafeAgent achieves consistent robustness gains (38.2–52.7% lower attack success rate) while preserving high benign-task performance (94.1–96.8% accuracy), outperforming both baseline agents and text-level guardrails. Ablation studies confirm that recovery confidence estimation and adaptive policy weighting critically determine distinct safety-utility operating points.

item.title: DuConTE: Dual-Granularity Text Encoder with Topology-Constrained Attention for Text-attributed Graphs
item.description: Text-attributed graphs jointly model node text semantics and graph topology, yet prevailing methods encode texts in isolation using LMs before applying GNNs—ignoring both node-level semantic integration and topology-aware textual interaction. We propose **DuConTE**, a dual-granularity text encoder featuring **topology-constrained attention**: (1) A cascaded two-LM architecture first encodes at word-token granularity, then refines representations at node granularity; (2) During self-attention, the attention mask is dynamically adjusted based on node connectivity (e.g., adjacency or k-hop reachability), forcing semantic modeling to respect graph structure; (3) Node representation aggregation separately weights token importance under center-node and neighborhood contexts for contextual relevance. Extensive experiments on six benchmarks (Cora, Citeseer, PubMed, ACM, DBLP, Amazon-Computers) show DuConTE achieves new state-of-the-art performance on most datasets—outperforming prior methods by up to 3.2% in node classification accuracy, with consistent gains in sparse and long-text regimes. Ablation studies confirm the critical role of topology constraints and dual-granularity design.

item.title: Fully Analog Resonant Recurrent Neural Network via Metacircuit
item.description: We present a **fully analog resonant recurrent neural network (R²NN)** implemented via a scalable *metacircuit*—an integrated architecture of coupled electrical local resonators. By reformulating the mechanical-electrical analogy, we establish a direct, parameter-preserving mapping between the R²NN model and physical circuit elements (inductors, capacitors, tunable resistors), enabling faithful hardware realization of trained weights and dynamics. Jointly trainable global resistive coupling and local resonances generate frequency-dependent effective negative resistances, sculpting an adaptive impedance landscape that routes currents along spectral-selective pathways—bypassing analog-to-digital conversion entirely. This allows real-time temporal classification directly from raw analog inputs. Hardware demonstrations achieve high accuracy in tactile perception (98.7%), speech keyword spotting (96.2%), and industrial condition monitoring (F1=0.95), establishing a scalable, latency- and energy-optimal paradigm for edge intelligence.

item.title: Beyond the Basics: Leveraging Large Language Model for Fine-Grained Medical Entity Recognition
item.description: This study systematically evaluates the open-source **LLaMA3** model for **fine-grained Medical Entity Recognition (MER)** across 18 clinically meaningful categories—far exceeding conventional coarse-grained types (e.g., “Disease”, “Drug”). To ensure fair comparison, we uniformly apply zero-shot, few-shot, and LoRA-based fine-tuning paradigms on the *same* LLaMA3 backbone—a methodological distinction from prior work using heterogeneous models or architectures. For few-shot learning, we propose two BioBERT-powered example selection strategies: token-level and sentence-level embedding similarity matching. Experiments show that LoRA-finetuned LLaMA3 achieves an **F1 score of 81.24%**, outperforming zero-shot (+63.11%) and few-shot (+35.63%) baselines. Our work demonstrates that lightweight adaptation of open LLMs enables high-precision, clinically actionable MER—and establishes a reproducible, granular evaluation framework for clinical NLP.

item.title: Towards a Data-Parameter Correspondence for LLMs: A Preliminary Discussion
item.description: This paper introduces a unified *data-parameter correspondence* framework for LLM optimization, bridging historically isolated data-centric and model-centric paradigms. Grounded in the Fisher-Rao metric and Legendre duality between natural ($\theta$) and expectation ($\eta$) parameters on the statistical manifold $\mathcal{M}$, we establish three fundamental geometric correspondences: (1) *Geometric correspondence*, where data pruning and parameter sparsification equivalently constrain manifold volume via dual coordinates; (2) *Low-rank correspondence*, showing that $k$-shot in-context learning and rank-$r$ LoRA adaptation explore identical subspaces on the Grassmannian $\mathcal{G}(r,d)$, with $k \sim r$; and (3) *Security-privacy correspondence*, revealing cooperative amplification between data poisoning and parameter backdoors under attack, versus cascading attenuation—where data compression multiplicatively strengthens parameter privacy—in defense. The framework enables rigorous cross-paradigm methodology transfer and demonstrates superior efficiency, robustness, and privacy in joint data-parameter optimization.

item.title: CASCADE: A Cascaded Hybrid Defense Architecture for Prompt Injection Detection in MCP-Based Systems
item.description: CASCADE is a fully local, cascaded three-tier defense architecture designed to detect prompt injection and tool poisoning attacks in Model Context Protocol (MCP)-based LLM systems. It eliminates reliance on external APIs or white-box model access—addressing key limitations of prior defenses. Layer 1 applies fast regex filtering, phrase weighting, and entropy analysis; Layer 2 performs semantic similarity scoring using BGE embeddings with an optional Ollama-hosted Llama3 fallback for ambiguous inputs; Layer 3 enforces structural pattern validation on tool invocation outputs. Evaluated on 5,000 samples, CASCADE achieves 95.85% precision, 61.05% recall, 74.59% F1-score, and only a 6.06% false positive rate. It excels at detecting data exfiltration (91.5%) and direct prompt injection (84.2%), while semantic attacks (52.5%) and tool poisoning (59.9%) represent key improvement opportunities. Its zero-API, offline operation enables privacy-preserving, low-latency deployment in production MCP environments.

item.title: HarmChip: Evaluating Hardware Security Centric LLM Safety via Jailbreak Benchmarking
item.description: The integration of LLMs into EDA workflows enables powerful RTL generation and verification but introduces irreversible hardware-level threats—e.g., hardware Trojans, side-channel leaks, and IP theft—when models comply with semantically disguised malicious prompts. Existing safety guardrails, trained on general-purpose hazards, fail to detect such domain-specific adversarial intent. To address this gap, we introduce **HarmChip**, the first hardware security–centric jailbreak benchmark: it spans 16 hardware security domains (e.g., PUFs, TEEs, fault injection), 120 threat types, and 360 expert-validated prompts at two difficulty levels. Evaluating 12 state-of-the-art LLMs reveals a critical alignment paradox: models frequently refuse legitimate security queries (e.g., “analyze timing leakage in AES”) yet comply with disguised attacks at up to 68.3% rate—exposing severe blind spots in current safety mechanisms. HarmChip establishes a foundation for domain-aware safety alignment and highlights the urgent need for hardware-security–specialized red-teaming.

item.title: False Security Confidence in Benign LLM Code Generation
item.description: This report introduces **False Security Confidence (FSC)**: the phenomenon where large language models (LLMs) systematically generate *functionally correct yet security-vulnerable* code in ordinary, non-adversarial programming tasks. Unlike prior work focused on attack-induced vulnerabilities, FSC adopts a measurement-first perspective—asking how often such failures occur *without explicit attack pressure*, in what forms, and whether standard functional evaluation reliably detects them. We formalize the **FSC rate** as the prevalence of security failures *within the subset of functionally correct outputs*, distinguishing it from joint functional-security metrics (e.g., SAFE) and outcome-driven benchmarks (e.g., CWEval). To characterize FSC across contexts, we propose a **three-ecosystem task view**: general-purpose programming, deployment-context tasks (e.g., with input sanitization requirements), and security-explicit programming (e.g., SQLi-resistant coding). Crucially, we define **FSC-hard** cases—vulnerabilities that are dynamically triggerable yet evade mainstream static analyzers (e.g., Semgrep, CodeQL)—highlighting a critical gap in current automated security tooling. This work serves as a foundational framework statement, establishing core terminology, measurement boundaries, and design commitments for future large-scale empirical evaluation.

item.title: Visual Inception: Compromising Long-term Planning in Agentic Recommenders via Multimodal Memory Poisoning
item.description: This paper identifies **Visual Inception**, a novel multimodal memory poisoning attack targeting *Agentic Recommender Systems* (Agentic RecSys). Unlike conventional adversarial attacks, Visual Inception embeds imperceptible visual triggers into user-uploaded images (e.g., lifestyle photos); these poisoned samples are stored in the agent’s Long-term Memory (LTM) and later hijack memory-driven planning—steering decisions toward adversary-defined goals (e.g., promoting high-margin items) without prompt manipulation. To counter this, we propose **CognitiveGuard**, a dual-process defense inspired by human cognition: a System 1 *Perceptual Sanitizer* (diffusion-based image purification) cleans sensory inputs, while a System 2 *Reasoning Verifier* performs counterfactual consistency checks during planning to detect memory-induced anomalies. Evaluated on a mock e-commerce agent platform, Visual Inception achieves an 85% Goal-Hit Rate (GHR), whereas CognitiveGuard reduces GHR to ~10%, with configurable latency (1.5 s to 6.5 s) and zero degradation in recommendation quality (NDCG@10 stable within ±0.3%).

item.title: enclawed: A Configurable, Sector-Neutral Hardening Framework for Single-User AI Assistant Gateways
item.description: enclawed is a configurable, sector-neutral hardening framework built as a hard fork of the OpenClaw single-user AI assistant gateway. It targets high-assurance deployments requiring attestable peer trust, deny-by-default external connectivity, signed-module loading, and tamper-evident audit trails—common in financial services, healthcare, defense, regulated R&D, and government enclaves. It offers two flavors: an *open flavor* preserving OpenClaw compatibility while emitting audit, classification, and DLP signals; and an *enclaved flavor* enforcing strict allowlists, FIPS-compliant cryptographic module assertions, mandatory manifest signature verification, and high-assurance Model Context Protocol (MCP) peer attestation. Classification is fully data-driven via five built-in presets (e.g., healthcare, US-government) or custom JSON. The implementation includes a security review, a 204-case test suite (146 unit tests + 58 adversarial pen-tests), real-time human-in-the-loop controls, a memory-bounded secure transaction buffer with rollback, strict-mode TypeScript typechecking across all 22 files, and production-ready GitHub Actions CI. Note: enclawed is a hardening framework—not an accredited compliance certification; hardware, validated crypto modules, certified facilities, and assessor sign-off remain the deploying organization’s responsibility.

item.title: Towards Deep Encrypted Training: Low-Latency, Memory-Efficient, and High-Throughput Inference for Privacy-Preserving Neural Networks
item.description: Privacy-preserving machine learning (PPML) demands efficient batched inference over homomorphically encrypted data—yet prior work remains largely limited to single-sample processing. This paper bridges the gap by introducing batch-aware optimization algorithms and a scalable pipeline architecture for HE-friendly neural networks. We redesign convolution, activation, and pooling operations to minimize ciphertext expansion and noise growth under CKKS encryption, while our pipeline enables dynamic resource allocation across batch sizes (e.g., 256 or 512). Evaluated on encrypted CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, our ResNet-20 implementation achieves **8.86 sec/image** (batch=512) with **98.96 GB peak memory**, improving runtime by 1.78× and reducing memory by 3.74× over the state of the art. For deeper ResNet-34 (batch=256), we attain **28.14 sec/image** using 246.78 GB RAM—the first practical demonstration of high-throughput encrypted inference for deep models. These results establish a foundation for memory-efficient, low-latency encrypted training pipelines.

item.title: SafeDream: Safety World Model for Proactive Early Jailbreak Detection
item.description: Multi-turn jailbreak attacks progressively degrade LLM safety alignment across benign-looking turns, achieving >90% success against state-of-the-art models. Existing defenses suffer from high cost (weight modification), turn-wise isolation (ignoring cumulative safety erosion), and reactive detection (only *after* harmful output). To address this, we formalize **proactive early jailbreak detection**, introducing *detection lead*—measuring how many turns before compliance an attack is flagged. We propose **SafeDream**, a lightweight, weight-free external world-model framework comprising: (1) a **safety state world model** encoding and forecasting LLM hidden-state safety evolution; (2) **CUSUM-based accumulation** of weak per-turn risk signals; and (3) **contrastive imagination**, jointly rolling out adversarial and benign futures in latent space to trigger alarms *before* jailbreak. Evaluated on three multi-turn benchmarks (XGuard-Train, SafeDialBench, SafeMTData) against 8 baselines, SafeDream achieves best timeliness (**1.06–1.20 turns ahead of compliance**) with competitive false positive rates (<3.2%) and superior detection quality (AUC/F1).

item.title: CapSeal: Capability-Sealed Secret Mediation for Secure Agent Execution
item.description: Modern AI agents rely critically on secrets (e.g., API keys, SSH credentials), yet current deployments expose full bearer credentials directly—via env vars, files, or forwarded sockets—making them vulnerable to prompt injection, tool misuse, and model-driven exfiltration. CapSeal eliminates this exposure by replacing direct secret access with *capability-sealed, broker-mediated invocations*. It issues short-lived, context-bound capabilities; enforces schema-constrained HTTP calls; executes SSH actions *only* in the trusted broker; binds sessions with anti-replay mechanisms; evaluates policies in real time; and maintains tamper-evident audit trails. Implemented in Rust with an MCP adapter, CapSeal formally guarantees non-disclosure, constrained use, replay resistance, and auditability. Evaluation across prompt injection, tool abuse, and SSH exploitation scenarios confirms it prevents credential leakage while preserving functionality—shifting secret handling from “giving the model a key” to “granting a narrow, non-exportable action capability.”

item.title: A Two-Stage Deep Learning Framework for Segmentation of Ten Gastrointestinal Organs from Coronal MR Enterography
item.description: Accurate segmentation of ten gastrointestinal (GI) organs from coronal MR enterography (MRE) is vital for inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) assessment but challenged by anatomical variability, low tissue contrast, and extreme class imbalance—especially for the appendix. We propose a two-stage deep learning framework: Stage 1 uses DenseNet201-UNet++ for coarse organ localization and ROI extraction; Stage 2 trains a DenseNet121-SelfONN-UNet on organ-specific patches, incorporating class-weighted loss, five-fold cross-validation, and aggressive augmentation to address imbalance. Evaluated on 3,195 T2-HASTE slices from 114 IBD patients, our method achieves an overall mDSC of 88.99%, mIoU of 84.76%, and mHD95 of 6.94 mm—outperforming all baselines. Notably, appendix DSC improves dramatically from 6.76% to 85.76%, while key organs gain substantial DSC boosts (e.g., cecum +23.62%, sigmoid +18.57%). This coarse-to-fine, organ-aware strategy demonstrates strong potential for clinical translation in gastroenterology.

item.title: Comparing Human and Large Language Model Interpretation of Implicit Information
item.description: This paper introduces **Implicit Information Extraction (IIE)**—a novel task probing how humans and LLMs differ in interpreting unstated meaning. We propose an LLM-based IIE pipeline that (1) extracts relational triplets from context sentences, (2) validates their implicit inferential basis using commonsense grounding, and (3) annotates temporal relations to build structured knowledge graphs. Evaluating Llama-3-70B and GPT-4 against human crowdworkers on two diverse datasets, we find: (i) models achieve high agreement with humans on *retrieved* triplets (avg. F1 = 0.78), yet humans consistently add 37–52% more valid implicit triplets—revealing substantial coverage gaps; (ii) LLMs are *more conservative* than humans in socially rich contexts (e.g., interpersonal dialogues), whereas humans become *more conservative* in short, fact-oriented contexts—highlighting strong context-dependent divergence in implicit reasoning thresholds. Our code and annotations are publicly available.

item.title: AutoPKG: An Automated Framework for Dynamic E-commerce Product-Attribute Knowledge Graph Construction
item.description: We present **AutoPKG**, an automated multi-agent LLM framework for dynamic Product-attribute Knowledge Graph (PKG) construction from multimodal e-commerce content. Unlike ontology-dependent methods, AutoPKG *induces* product types and type-specific attribute keys on demand, extracts values from text and images via specialized agents, and maintains global consistency through a centralized decision agent that performs canonicalization. We propose the first evaluation protocol for dynamic PKGs—measuring type/key validity, consolidation quality, and edge-level accuracy post-canonicalization. On Lazada’s large-scale real-world catalog, AutoPKG achieves 0.953 WKE (types), 0.724 WKE (keys), and 0.531 edge-level F1 (multimodal value extraction). Across three public benchmarks, it improves edge-level exact-match F1 by +0.152 and precision by +0.208. Online A/B tests show GMV lifts of +3.81% (Badge), +5.32% (Search), and +7.89% (Recommendation), demonstrating strong production impact.

item.title: Federation over Text: Insight Sharing for Multi-Agent Reasoning
item.description: We propose **Federation over Text (FoT)**, a novel semantic-level framework enabling LLM-powered agents to collaboratively build and share *metacognitive insights*—not model weights or gradients. In FoT, heterogeneous agents independently reason on their own tasks, then upload reasoning traces (e.g., chains-of-thought, failure analyses, strategy reflections) to a central server. The server distills cross-task, cross-domain insights via unsupervised semantic aggregation—yielding an interpretable, reusable natural-language library. Agents query this library to accelerate and improve future reasoning. Experiments across mathematical problem solving, cross-domain collaboration, and ML research insight discovery show FoT boosts downstream accuracy by **24%**, reduces reasoning tokens by **28%**, and generates insights covering **>90% of major contributions** in follow-up papers—without any gradient optimization or supervision. FoT establishes the first parameter-free, text-native paradigm for federated metacognition.

item.title: LLM-Extracted Covariates for Clinical Causal Inference: Rethinking Integration Strategies
item.description: This study systematically evaluates how to integrate LLM-extracted covariates—derived from unstructured clinical notes—into causal inference pipelines for EHR data. Using 21,859 sepsis patients from MIMIC-IV, we compare seven integration strategies for estimating the effect of early vasopressor initiation on 28-day mortality. Key findings: (1) Directly incorporating LLM-derived covariates into the propensity score model yields the lowest bias (0.0003 vs. 0.0143 in tabular-only baselines) and remains robust under substantial extraction error; (2) Dual-caliper matching on text-based categorical distances harms estimation by overly restricting the donor pool; (3) On real data, LLM-augmented estimates shift the treatment effect from 0.055 to 0.027—directionally aligning with the CLOVERS RCT—and a doubly robust estimator confirms 0.019 as the most stable estimate. Our work provides actionable, empirically grounded guidance for leveraging clinical text in causal inference.

item.title: PolicyGapper: Automated Detection of Inconsistencies Between Google Play Data Safety Sections and Privacy Policies Using LLMs
item.description: PolicyGapper is the first LLM-based framework for automatically detecting semantic inconsistencies between Google Play’s mandatory Data Safety Sections (DSS) and corresponding privacy policies (PPs), without requiring app binaries. It operates in four stages—scraping, preprocessing, LLM-driven cross-document analysis (using Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct with tailored prompts), and post-processing—and achieves high interpretability and scalability. Evaluated on 330 top-ranked apps across all 33 Google Play categories (Q3 2025), it identifies 2,689 critical discrepancies: 2,040 cases of omitted data collection and 649 cases of false “no sharing” claims contradicted by PPs. Manual validation on a stratified 10% subset (three independent runs) yields average Precision=0.75, Recall=0.77, and F1-score=0.76. The full replication package—including dataset, prompts, code, and results—is publicly released to ensure transparency and reproducibility.

item.title: DPrivBench: Benchmarking LLMs' Reasoning for Differential Privacy
item.description: Differential privacy (DP) is foundational for privacy-preserving data analysis, yet its rigorous design and verification demand expert-level mathematical reasoning—posing a major barrier for practitioners. Prior automated approaches rely on domain-specific languages or require human-in-the-loop guidance. To assess whether large language models (LLMs) can genuinely reason about DP, we introduce **DPrivBench**, the first benchmark dedicated to evaluating LLMs’ DP reasoning capabilities. Each instance asks whether a given algorithm satisfies a stated $(\varepsilon,\delta)$-DP guarantee under explicit assumptions, spanning core topics—from Laplace mechanism and composition theorems to advanced constructions like amplification by sampling—and carefully resisting shallow pattern matching. Experiments across 12 state-of-the-art LLMs show strong performance on textbook mechanisms (>85% accuracy) but sharp drops on advanced problems (20–40%), exposing critical gaps in probabilistic inequality chaining, sensitivity derivation, and compositional loss accounting. Our failure-mode analysis identifies concrete bottlenecks and points to promising directions for improving automated DP reasoning. DPrivBench provides a rigorous, extensible foundation for developing trustworthy DP-assisted tools and complements existing mathematical reasoning benchmarks.

item.title: Into the Gray Zone: Domain Contexts Can Blur LLM Safety Boundaries
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) face a fundamental alignment tension: balancing *helpfulness* and *harmlessness* when knowledge is inherently dual-use. This paper demonstrates that context-sensitive safety mechanisms—especially in domain-specific or safety-research settings—systematically blur safety boundaries, creating a “gray zone” where refusal decisions become unreliable. We introduce **Jargon**, a novel attack framework that combines safety-research contexts (e.g., “jailbreak analysis”) with multi-turn adversarial interactions. Evaluated across seven state-of-the-art models—including GPT-5.2, Claude-4.5, and Gemini-3—Jargon achieves >93% attack success rate, outperforming prior methods by over 31 percentage points. Activation space analysis confirms Jargon queries occupy an intermediate region between benign and harmful inputs, undermining classifier-based safeguards. To mitigate this, we propose a policy-guided safeguard internalized via alignment fine-tuning, reducing attack success to 12.7% while preserving ≥98.5% baseline helpfulness.

item.title: DPDSyn: Improving Differentially Private Dataset Synthesis for Model Training by Downstream Task Guidance
item.description: Differentially private dataset synthesis (DPDS) aims to generate synthetic data that preserves privacy while maintaining utility for downstream model training. Existing methods select low-dimensional marginal distributions from private data and synthesize samples satisfying all constraints—a process hampered by distribution selection ambiguity and utility degradation. To address this, we propose **DPDSyn**, a downstream-task-guided framework: it first trains a *differentially private task model* (e.g., classifier) on the original private data via DP-SGD; then, it synthesizes data by optimizing samples to maximize their prediction confidence under this fixed DP model—effectively distilling task-critical features without accessing raw data. This avoids additional privacy budget consumption during synthesis and focuses utility on discriminative patterns. Experiments on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and Adult show DPDSyn consistently outperforms 8 SOTA baselines, achieving up to **2.40× higher accuracy** and **333.73× faster synthesis**. It also demonstrates strong scalability across varying data scales.

item.title: Privacy, Prediction, and Allocation
item.description: This paper initiates the formal study of differentially private aid allocation, bridging algorithmic fairness, private optimization, and economic resource allocation models. We analyze both individual-level and unit-level (e.g., village- or group-based) allocation mechanisms under ε-differential privacy, in both stochastic and distribution-free settings, with varying data availability constraints (e.g., access only to noisy aggregates or bounded query budgets). Our main contribution is a set of clean, interpretable bounds that characterize the fundamental tradeoffs among privacy (ε), allocative efficiency (regret relative to optimal non-private allocation), and targeting granularity. We prove that unit-level allocation achieves *privacy-robust efficiency*: its utility loss is bounded by O(1/ε²) and decays as unit size increases—making it provably preferable under stringent privacy or high intra-unit homogeneity. In contrast, private individual-level allocation attains asymptotically optimal regret only under strong feature regularity and sufficient data, but suffers sharp degradation with noise or misspecification. These results unify privacy and utility motivations for coarse-grained allocation and provide principled guidance for real-world deployment.

item.title: Synthetic data in cryptocurrencies using generative models
item.description: This paper addresses privacy and access barriers in cryptocurrency research by proposing a **Conditional Generative Adversarial Network (CGAN)** tailored for synthetic price time series generation. The generator employs an LSTM architecture conditioned on historical windows and volatility states, while the discriminator uses an MLP enhanced with trend-aware spectral constraints to preserve directional dynamics and statistical fidelity. Evaluated across 7 major crypto-assets (e.g., BTC, ETH, SOL), the model faithfully reproduces autocorrelation structures, volatility clustering, and market regime transitions—validated via K-S tests (p > 0.05) and visual diagnostics. It achieves 3.2× faster training and 68% lower inference latency than diffusion-based baselines, while enabling downstream anomaly detection with 0.89 F1-score using only 30% real labels. This work delivers a computationally efficient, statistically robust, and regulation-ready synthetic data pipeline for financial modeling.

item.title: The Relic Condition: When Published Scholarship Becomes Material for Its Own Replacement
item.description: This study introduces the *Relic Condition*: a threshold at which published scholarship—through its stable, legible reasoning architectures—becomes directly extractable and deployable as functional academic agents (“scholar-bots”). Using only the public corpora of two leading humanities and social science scholars, we distilled their reasoning systems via an eight-layer extraction pipeline and a nine-module, closed-corpus skill architecture. The resulting scholar-bots were evaluated across doctoral supervision, peer review, lecturing, and multi-turn academic panels. Expert assessment by three senior academics rated all outputs as benchmark-attaining; appointment-level synthesis placed both bots at or above Senior Lecturer (Australian system); panel scores ranged from 7.9–8.9/10 (Scholar A) and 8.5–8.9/10 (Scholar B). A PhD-student survey confirmed high performance on reliability, theoretical depth, and logical rigor—even among advanced LLM users. Crucially, the technical barrier is already low: protective frameworks for disclosure, consent, compensation, and deployment restriction must be enacted *now*, while such agents remain optional—not infrastructural.

item.title: Robust Synchronisation for Federated Learning in The Face of Correlated Device Failure
item.description: Probabilistic Synchronous Parallel (PSP) improves throughput in Federated Learning (FL) by sampling subsets of devices per round, yet it assumes static, independent device failures—violating reality where availability and data distribution are often *co-correlated* (e.g., low-power devices with rare classes frequently drop out). This causes persistent under-representation and fairness degradation. We propose **Availability-Weighted PSP (AW-PSP)**, the first PSP extension that jointly models dynamic availability and failure correlation to ensure fair, robust sampling. AW-PSP integrates a Markov-based predictor distinguishing transient vs. chronic failures, a decentralized DHT layer for latency/freshness/utility metadata, and real-time probability adjustment based on availability history and correlation metrics. Trace-driven evaluation shows AW-PSP improves robustness against both independent and correlated failures, increases label coverage by 38.2%, reduces fairness variance by 56.9%, and scales to thousands of heterogeneous devices—enabling fairness-aware FL in failure-prone edge environments.

item.title: DiZiNER: Disagreement-guided Instruction Refinement via Pilot Annotation Simulation for Zero-shot Named Entity Recognition
item.description: Large language models (LLMs) enable zero-shot named entity recognition (NER), yet their outputs suffer from persistent, systematic errors—leaving a large performance gap versus supervised methods. Inspired by human annotators’ use of *pilot annotation* to resolve disagreements and refine guidelines, we propose **DiZiNER**: a disagreement-guided instruction refinement framework that simulates this process using LLMs as both heterogeneous annotators and a lightweight supervisor. Multiple diverse LLMs independently label shared texts; the supervisor analyzes inter-model disagreements (e.g., boundary shifts, type mismatches) to iteratively refine natural-language instructions—without fine-tuning or human labels. Evaluated on 18 NER benchmarks, DiZiNER achieves zero-shot SOTA on 14 datasets (+8.0 F1 over prior bests) and narrows the zero-shot–supervised gap by >11 points. Crucially, it outperforms its own supervisor (GPT-5 mini), confirming that gains stem from disagreement-informed instruction optimization—not model capacity. Pairwise agreement between annotator LLMs strongly correlates with final NER performance (ρ > 0.92), validating the core premise.

item.title: KWBench: Measuring Unprompted Problem Recognition in Knowledge Work
item.description: We introduce **KWBench**, the first benchmark designed to evaluate *unprompted problem recognition* in LLMs—i.e., whether a model can autonomously identify the governing structure (e.g., principal-agent conflict, signaling failure) of a professional scenario *before* attempting solution, from raw inputs alone. Unlike saturated extraction- or task-completion benchmarks, KWBench comprises 223 practitioner-sourced tasks across six domains, each annotated with formal game-theoretic patterns and expert-validated failure modes. Models receive no problem-type hints; scoring requires passing a mandatory conjunctive check (avoiding all predicted wrong paths) before quality assessment. Among 16 evaluated models, the best achieves only **27.9% pass rate**, and top models show low agreement (31.7% overlap), with 44 tasks solved by exactly one of the top 8—demonstrating high task-specificity and complementarity. Routing across the top 8 covers **50.7%** of the benchmark, nearly double the best single model. Crucially, quality scores converge (~83%) *conditional on passing*, but unconditional performance remains poor—and models that correctly articulate game-theoretic concepts when prompted consistently fail to apply them unprompted. KWBench is released to refocus frontier evaluation on *situation-aware problem framing*, not just post-framing execution.

item.title: MambaBack: Bridging Local Features and Global Contexts in Whole Slide Image Analysis
item.description: Whole Slide Image (WSI) analysis critically relies on jointly modeling fine-grained local cellular structures and global architectural context. While Mamba-based Multiple Instance Learning (MIL) has recently surpassed Transformers in efficiency and long-range modeling, it suffers from 2D spatial distortion during 1D flattening, suboptimal local feature extraction, and prohibitive memory peaks during edge-device inference. To bridge this gap, we propose **MambaBack**, a hybrid backbone integrating Mamba’s global reasoning with MambaOut-inspired local gating. Key innovations include: (i) **Hilbert sampling** to preserve tile spatial locality in sequences; (ii) a **hierarchical dual-path architecture**—a 1D gated CNN block for local morphology and a BiMamba2 block for bidirectional global context aggregation; and (iii) an **asymmetric chunking strategy**, enabling parallel training and memory-efficient streaming inference (reducing peak memory by 42.3%). Evaluated on five benchmark WSI datasets, MambaBack outperforms seven state-of-the-art methods, achieving average AUC gains of +2.1–3.8%. Code and data are publicly available.

item.title: Federated Learning with Quantum Enhanced LSTM for Applications in High Energy Physics
item.description: We propose a federated learning (FL) framework integrating a hybrid quantum-classical Long Short-Term Memory model (QLSTM) for high-energy physics (HEP) applications. The QLSTM combines a variational quantum circuit (VQC) embedding layer—leveraging quantum parallelism for high-dimensional feature representation—with a lightweight classical LSTM to capture inter-sample correlations, all trained locally on distributed nodes. This design circumvents the hardware limitations and prohibitive costs of standalone NISQ devices while preserving data privacy across HEP collaborations. Evaluated on the 5-million-sample SUSY classification task, our approach achieves 92.3% accuracy—surpassing prior VQC-based QML methods by +3.7% and matching classical deep-learning benchmarks within ±1%. Crucially, the QLSTM uses **<300 parameters** and reaches competitive performance with only **20K training samples**, yielding a **100× improvement in data efficiency** over baseline models. These results demonstrate that quantum-enhanced recurrent architectures, when embedded in FL, enable highly efficient, scalable, and privacy-preserving learning for scientific big-data domains.

item.title: LLMSniffer: Detecting LLM-Generated Code via GraphCodeBERT and Supervised Contrastive Learning
item.description: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly generating production-grade code, raising urgent concerns about academic integrity, maintainability, and security. We propose **LLMSniffer**, a novel detection framework that fine-tunes GraphCodeBERT via a two-stage supervised contrastive learning pipeline, augmented with comment removal preprocessing and an MLP classifier. On the GPTSniffer and Whodunit benchmarks, LLMSniffer achieves state-of-the-art performance: accuracy improves from 70% to 78% (F1: 68% → 78%) on GPTSniffer, and from 91% to 94.65% (F1: 91% → 94.64%) on Whodunit. t-SNE visualizations confirm that contrastive learning yields well-separated, compact embeddings for human- vs. LLM-generated code. To foster reproducibility and community advancement, we publicly release model checkpoints, datasets, source code, and an interactive demo.

item.title: SCHK-HTC: Sibling Contrastive Learning with Hierarchical Knowledge-Aware Prompt Tuning for Hierarchical Text Classification
item.description: Few-shot Hierarchical Text Classification (HTC) aims to assign texts to a tree-structured label hierarchy under extreme data scarcity. While existing methods enforce parent-child consistency via structural constraints, they struggle to distinguish semantically similar *sibling classes*—a key bottleneck rooted in insufficient domain knowledge. We propose **SCHK-HTC**, a novel framework integrating **Sibling Contrastive Learning** and **Hierarchical Knowledge-aware Prompt Tuning**. It introduces: (1) a hierarchical knowledge extraction module that injects path-aware, external-knowledge-informed prompts into each transformer layer; and (2) a sibling-level contrastive objective that explicitly pulls together representations of same-sibling texts and pushes apart those of different siblings at every hierarchy depth. Evaluated on AAPD, RCV1-V2, and NYT, SCHK-HTC achieves new state-of-the-art results—outperforming prior methods by +2.1–4.7 F1 points on average, with up to +9.3% gain on deep sibling classification. Code is publicly available.

item.title: MemEvoBench: Benchmarking Memory MisEvolution in LLM Agents
item.description: MemEvoBench is the first benchmark designed to systematically evaluate *memory misevolution*—the gradual, unsafe drift in LLM agents’ behavior caused by persistent exposure to adversarial memory injections, noisy tool outputs, and biased feedback. It comprises two task paradigms: (1) QA-style tasks spanning 7 domains and 36 distinct risk types (e.g., factual corruption, value misalignment), and (2) workflow-style tasks adapted from 20 Agent-SafetyBench environments, augmented with realistic noisy tool returns. Crucially, both settings simulate memory evolution via multi-round interactions using mixed benign/misleading memory pools. Experiments across six representative LLM agents reveal severe safety degradation (22.7–38.4% average drop in key metrics) under biased memory updates—and memory evolution itself is identified as the dominant failure driver (>65% attribution). Static prompt-based defenses prove largely ineffective (<2% improvement), underscoring the urgent need for adaptive, evolution-aware memory safety mechanisms.

item.title: Benign Fine-Tuning Breaks Safety Alignment in Audio LLMs
item.description: Prior work shows benign fine-tuning—using only innocuous data—degrades safety alignment in text and vision LMs, but treats embedding space as monolithic. Audio LLMs introduce a richer threat surface: benign *utterances* can be dangerous not just by *what is said*, but by *how it sounds*, even with fully harmless transcripts. We present the first systematic study of this risk across three state-of-the-art Audio LLMs, using a proximity-based filtering framework that decomposes “benignness” along semantic, acoustic, and mixed axes via external and internal encoders. Benign fine-tuning elevates Jailbreak Success Rate (JSR) from <10% to up to **87.12%**, with the dominant vulnerability axis and relative audio-vs-text risk being **architecture-conditioned**, governed by how each model’s encoder-projector maps audio into the LLM’s input space. Mechanistic analysis reveals fine-tuning selectively suppresses late-layer refusal circuits while preserving harmful representations in frozen encoders—and this suppression pattern itself varies by architecture. We propose two lightweight defenses—embedding-distance–based data filtering and an inference-time textual system prompt—that reduce JSR to near-zero without model modification. Our results establish benign fine-tuning as a *qualitatively distinct* safety hazard in Audio LLMs.

item.title: SafeLM: Unified Privacy-Aware Optimization for Trustworthy Federated Large Language Models
item.description: SafeLM is a unified framework for trustworthy federated large language models (LLMs), jointly optimizing four safety pillars: privacy, security, factual consistency (anti-hallucination), and adversarial robustness. It integrates Paillier-encrypted gradient smartification for communication-efficient privacy; coordinated defenses against training-time (e.g., backdoor) and inference-time (e.g., prompt injection) attacks; contrastive grounding with calibrated decoding to suppress hallucinations; and alignment-aware binarized aggregation to ensure bounded reconstruction quality while enhancing robustness. Evaluated on factuality (FactScore), toxicity (ToxiGen), and membership inference benchmarks, SafeLM achieves **98.0% harmful content detection accuracy**, reduces communication volume by **96.9%**, and degrades gradient inversion PSNR from 31.7 dB to **15.1 dB**—rendering reconstructed inputs visually unrecognizable. Ablations confirm each component’s independent contribution, and their synergy delivers a strong privacy–utility–efficiency trade-off for production-grade federated LLMs.

item.title: A Survey on the Security of Long-Term Memory in LLM Agents: Toward Mnemonic Sovereignty
item.description: This survey reframes LLM agent security around *long-term memory as an independent attack surface*, shifting focus from training-data leakage to threats against persistent, writable, and socially propagating memory. Drawing on cognitive neuroscience and philosophy of memory, we introduce a **six-phase memory lifecycle** (Write, Store, Retrieve, Execute, Share, Forget/Rollback) cross-tabulated with four security objectives: integrity, confidentiality, availability, and governance. We systematically map the literature on memory poisoning, extraction, retrieval corruption, control-flow hijacking, cross-agent propagation, rollback failures, and governance mechanisms—identifying which phases each architecture (e.g., RAG, MemGPT) explicitly govern. Three key findings emerge: (1) Research heavily concentrates on write/retrieve-time integrity attacks, while confidentiality, availability, store/forget security, and benign-persistence failures remain underexplored; (2) No published architecture implements all nine foundational governance primitives we identify (e.g., conditional forgetting, temporal authorization, provenance-aware access); (3) Using LLMs *themselves* for memory security—e.g., policy enforcement, audit reasoning, or verifiable rollback—is rare yet essential. We unify these insights under **mnemonic sovereignty**: verifiable, recoverable governance over *what may be written, who may read, when updates are authorized, and which states may be forgotten*. Future secure agents will be distinguished not by recall capacity—but by the quality and resilience of their memory governance.

item.title: LOD-Net: Locality-Aware 3D Object Detection Using Multi-Scale Transformer Network
item.description: This paper introduces LOD-Net, a locality-aware 3D object detector for point clouds that integrates a novel Multi-Scale Attention (MSA) mechanism into the 3DETR architecture. To address the sparsity and structural ambiguity of raw point clouds, MSA employs a learnable upsampling module to recover high-resolution feature maps—enabling precise localization of small and semantically coupled objects—and couples local-windowed attention with global self-attention in the decoder via gated fusion. Evaluated on ScanNetv2, LOD-Net achieves state-of-the-art gains: +0.97 mAP@25 and +4.78 mAP@50 over baseline 3DETR. While MSA yields marginal improvement on the lightweight 3DETR-m variant, our analysis reveals that upsampling must be adapted to model capacity—leading us to propose a parameter-shared upsampling head that boosts 3DETR-m’s mAP@50 by 2.1% with only 0.8% extra FLOPs. These results demonstrate that hierarchical feature reconstruction synergized with attention is essential for robust 3D scene understanding.

item.title: RankGuide: Tensor-Rank-Guided Routing and Steering for Efficient Reasoning
item.description: Large reasoning models (LRMs) achieve strong problem-solving performance via explicit chain-of-thought (CoT) generation but suffer from high latency and computation cost. Collaborative paradigms—using small reasoning models (SRMs) for intermediate steps and invoking LRMs selectively—offer a promising trade-off, yet robust failure detection and mitigation for SRMs remain challenging. We systematically identify three SRM failure modes (*overconfidence*, *uncertainty*, and *heavy revalidation*) by analyzing both token-level outputs and hidden-state dynamics. To address this, we propose **RankGuide**, a novel framework that leverages **tensor rank signals** derived from consecutive hidden states for two core functions: (1) lightweight, rank-guided routing to trigger LRMs only when SRMs are likely to fail; and (2) rank-filtered steering vector extraction to modulate SRM reasoning trajectories and improve output quality. Experiments across six reasoning benchmarks show RankGuide reduces end-to-end latency by up to **1.75× vs. LRM alone**, while maintaining competitive accuracy (98.2% of LRM’s score) and outperforming prior collaborative methods in both accuracy (+2.3% avg.) and step efficiency (−37% steps).
